name.text: Week 13 – Q2: Lossless Transmission Line Parameters

questiontext.text:
Lossless Transmission Line Parameters

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 7 points (7 parts, 1 pt each) | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A lossless coaxial transmission line has a dielectric material ({@mat_name@}) with relative permittivity \(\varepsilon_r = {@eps_r_disp@}\) and distributed capacitance \(C' = {@Cp_disp@}\;\text{pF/m}\). The line operates at frequency \(f = {@f_disp_MHz@}\;\text{MHz}\).

For a lossless line, the phase velocity is \(v_p = c_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\), where \(c_0 = 3 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}\). Determine the following line parameters.

Part (a) — Phase velocity (1 pt)

Compute the phase velocity \(v_p = c_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\). Give your answer in m/s.

\(v_p = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] m/s

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 2.07e8 or 207000000

Part (b) — Distributed inductance (1 pt)

Using \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\), compute the distributed inductance \(L'\). Give your answer in H/m.

\(L' = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]] H/m

Syntax hint: Enter in H/m, e.g. 2.5e-7

Part (c) — Characteristic impedance (1 pt)

Compute the characteristic impedance \(Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'}\). Give your answer in Ω.

\(Z_0 = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] Ω

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 50 or 7.25e1

Part (d) — Wavelength (1 pt)

Compute the wavelength \(\lambda = v_p / f\). Give your answer in metres.

\(\lambda = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] m

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.414 or 4.14e-1

Part (e) — Phase constant (1 pt)

Compute the phase constant \(\beta = 2\pi / \lambda\). Give your answer in rad/m.

\(\beta = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]] rad/m

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 15.2 or 1.52e1. You may also use %pi for π, e.g. 2*%pi/0.414.

Part (f) — Dispersion (1 pt)

Is a lossless transmission line dispersive?

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Select Yes or No from the dropdown.

Part (g) — Effect of doubling εr on Z0 (1 pt)

If the relative permittivity \(\varepsilon_r\) is doubled (while \(C'\) remains the same), what happens to \(Z_0\)?

[[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Select from the dropdown.

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Part (a) — Phase velocity

The phase velocity on a lossless line filled with dielectric of relative permittivity \(\varepsilon_r\) is:

\[v_p = \frac{c_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{{@eps_r_disp@}}} = {@ta1@}\;\text{m/s}\]

Part (b) — Distributed inductance

From \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\), we solve for \(L'\):

\[L' = \frac{1}{v_p^2 \, C'} = \frac{1}{({@ta1@})^2 \times {@Cp@}} = {@ta2@}\;\text{H/m} \approx {@ta2_nH@}\;\text{nH/m}\]

Part (c) — Characteristic impedance

The characteristic impedance of a lossless line is:

\[Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L'}{C'}} = \sqrt{\frac{{@ta2@}}{{@Cp@}}} = {@ta3@}\;\Omega\]

Part (d) — Wavelength

The wavelength at frequency \(f\) is:

\[\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{{@ta1@}}{{@f_Hz@}} = {@ta4@}\;\text{m}\]

Part (e) — Phase constant

The phase constant is:

\[\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{{@ta4@}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{rad/m}\]

Part (f) — Dispersion

No. A lossless transmission line is non-dispersive. The phase velocity \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\) is independent of frequency, so all frequency components travel at the same speed. Dispersion occurs only when the line has losses (especially frequency-dependent losses).

Part (g) — Effect of doubling εr

Increases. The characteristic impedance can be written as:

\[Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L'}{C'}} = \frac{1}{v_p \, C'} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c_0 \, C'}\]

Since \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\), we have \(Z_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/(c_0 C')\). If \(\varepsilon_r\) doubles (with \(C'\) constant), \(Z_0\) increases by a factor of \(\sqrt{2}\).

Answer Summary

PartQuantityAnswer
(a)\(v_p\){@ta1@} m/s
(b)\(L'\){@ta2@} H/m ({@ta2_nH@} nH/m)
(c)\(Z_0\){@ta3@} Ω
(d)\(\lambda\){@ta4@} m
(e)\(\beta\){@ta5@} rad/m
(f)Dispersive?No
(g)\(\varepsilon_r\) doubles → \(Z_0\)Increases

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Phase velocity

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Distributed inductance

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — Characteristic impedance

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Wavelength

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — Phase constant

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (f) — Dispersion

[[feedback:prt6]]

Part (g) — Effect of doubling εr

[[feedback:prt7]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: {@ mat_name @}, εr={@eps_r_disp@}, C'={@Cp_disp@} pF/m, f={@f_disp_MHz@} MHz | vp={@ta1@} m/s, L'={@ta2@} H/m, Z0={@ta3@} Ω, λ={@ta4@} m, β={@ta5@} rad/m

questiondescription.text: Lossless transmission line parameters — Week 13 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: Correct! The phase velocity is \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r} = {@ta1@}\;\text{m/s}\). In a dielectric-filled line, the wave travels slower than the speed of light by a factor of \(\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Use \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r} = 3\times10^8 / \sqrt{{@eps_r_disp@}} = {@ta1@}\;\text{m/s}\). The dielectric slows the wave by a factor of \(\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! \(L' = 1/(v_p^2 C') = {@ta2@}\;\text{H/m}\) (approximately {@ta2_nH@} nH/m). The distributed inductance and capacitance together determine both the wave speed and impedance.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. From \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\), solve for \(L' = 1/(v_p^2 \cdot C')\). Remember to convert \(C'\) from pF/m to F/m: \({@Cp_disp@}\;\text{pF/m} = {@Cp@}\;\text{F/m}\). The answer is \(L' = {@ta2@}\;\text{H/m}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! \(Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'} = {@ta3@}\;\Omega\). The characteristic impedance is a real number for a lossless line, meaning voltage and current are in phase for a travelling wave.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Use \(Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'} = \sqrt{{@ta2@}/{@Cp@}} = {@ta3@}\;\Omega\). For a lossless line, \(Z_0\) is purely real.

truefeedback.text: Correct! \(\lambda = v_p/f = {@ta4@}\;\text{m}\). The wavelength on the line is shorter than the free-space wavelength by a factor of \(\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Use \(\lambda = v_p/f = {@ta1@}/{@f_Hz@} = {@ta4@}\;\text{m}\). Make sure the frequency is in Hz (not MHz).

truefeedback.text: Correct! \(\beta = 2\pi/\lambda = {@ta5@}\;\text{rad/m}\). The phase constant tells you how many radians of phase shift occur per metre of line.

Correct! A lossless transmission line is non-dispersive. The phase velocity \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\) does not depend on frequency, so all spectral components of a signal travel at the same speed and the waveform shape is preserved.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. A lossless line is non-dispersive because \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\) is independent of frequency. Dispersion only arises when the propagation parameters (R', G') introduce frequency-dependent losses.

node.description: Check part (g): effect of doubling eps_r

truefeedback.text: Correct! When \(\varepsilon_r\) doubles, \(v_p\) decreases (since \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\)), which increases \(L' = 1/(v_p^2 C')\). Since \(C'\) is unchanged, \(Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'}\) increases. Equivalently, \(Z_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/(c_0 C')\), which grows with \(\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Write \(Z_0 = 1/(v_p C') = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/(c_0 C')\). Since \(C'\) is held constant and \(Z_0 \propto \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\), doubling \(\varepsilon_r\) increases \(Z_0\) by a factor of \(\sqrt{2}\). The key insight is that a higher \(\varepsilon_r\) slows the wave, increasing the per-unit-length inductance while capacitance is fixed.

hint.text: Hint 1: Start with the phase velocity: for a lossless line in a medium with relative permittivity \(\varepsilon_r\), the wave travels at \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\). This is slower than the speed of light because the dielectric stores energy in its electric field, effectively slowing the wave.

Hint 2: The key relationships for a lossless transmission line are: \(v_p = 1/\sqrt{L'C'}\) and \(Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'}\). From the first, you can find \(L'\) once you know \(v_p\) and \(C'\). The wavelength is \(\lambda = v_p/f\) and the phase constant is \(\beta = 2\pi/\lambda = \omega/v_p\).

Hint 3: For the conceptual parts: a lossless line has frequency-independent \(v_p\), making it non-dispersive. For part (g), express \(Z_0\) in terms of \(\varepsilon_r\): since \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\) and \(L' = 1/(v_p^2 C')\), we get \(Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'} = 1/(v_p C') = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/(c_0 C')\). So \(Z_0\) grows as \(\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\) when \(C'\) is held constant.
